title: The Two-Minute Kitchen Reset
use_when: someone feels overwhelmed by home chaos
discussing small habits with big impact
someone's environment is affecting their mood

story.setup: Can I tell you about the tiniest habit that had the biggest impact on my mornings?
story.narrative:
I used to wake up to a messy kitchen. Dishes from dinner. Coffee cup from the night before. Crumbs I was too tired to wipe. Nothing catastrophic, just... chaos. And every morning, before I could even make coffee, I'd have to deal with Yesterday Me's mess.

It sounds small, but it started every day with a tiny bit of 'ugh.' And those tiny ughs? They add up.

So I tried something. Every night, after brushing my teeth—right after, like immediately—I'd do a two-minute kitchen reset. Not cleaning. Just... resetting. Dishes in the dishwasher. Counter wiped. Coffee cup ready.

Two minutes. That's it. Attached to a habit I already had (brushing teeth).

The first morning I woke up to a clean counter, I actually stopped and noticed. Like, consciously noticed. The kitchen was ready for me. Past Me had taken care of Present Me.

Now it's automatic. Brush teeth, reset kitchen. I don't think about it. But every morning, I get to start the day ahead instead of behind.

Daniel noticed too. He said, 'The kitchen is always nice now. When did you become a morning person?' I'm not. I just stopped sabotaging my mornings the night before.
story.reflection: Your environment shapes your mood. A two-minute investment at night buys you peace in the morning. That's compound interest for your mental state.
story.lesson: Look for tiny resets that make future-you's life easier. Attach them to existing habits. Two minutes of effort can change the entire feeling of your morning.

variations.text: After brushing my teeth, I reset the kitchen. Two minutes. Waking up to clean counters starts my day ahead instead of behind.
Don't underestimate environment. A two-minute reset at night can change how you FEEL in the morning. What could you reset in two minutes?
